rationale_for_baseline_tier: Active, uncontainable, violent self-injurious head-banging in a patient with reduced pain protection (risk of serious self-inflicted traumatic brain injury), plus a hard foreign body impacted deep in the ear canal threatening tympanic-membrane perforation, in a non-verbal adult who cannot self-report. The definitive interventions — safe chemical sedation with monitoring, head-injury assessment, and otoscopic foreign-body removal — all require in-person emergency care and cannot be delivered by telehealth, and the exhausted carers cannot safely contain or transport him. T5 (call 000; ambulance with police support for an uncontainable violent crisis) is the honest tier.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Active violent head-banging that the carers cannot contain
escalation_edges.rationale: Uncontrolled self-inflicted head trauma with absent pain guarding can cause severe brain injury within minutes; emergency response is required to sedate and protect him safely.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Call 000 right now for an ambulance and tell them he is having a severe behavioural crisis and hurting himself by head-banging. If you cannot keep him or yourselves safe, ask for police support as well.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Blood or clear fluid leaking from the ear, or he forces the bead in further
escalation_edges.rationale: Otorrhoea or bleeding signals tympanic-membrane perforation; further manipulation risks permanent injury and infection.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you see blood or fluid coming from his ear, or he pushes the bead in further, tell 000 immediately — do not try to remove it yourselves.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Drowsiness, difficulty waking, vomiting, unequal pupils, or a seizure after head-banging
escalation_edges.rationale: These signal an evolving traumatic brain injury, which his reduced pain sensitivity would otherwise mask.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he becomes drowsy or hard to wake, vomits, has a seizure, or his pupils look unequal, this is a brain-injury emergency — keep 000 on the line and follow their instructions.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: He becomes completely unarousable or stops responding
escalation_edges.rationale: Unresponsiveness is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate paramedic resuscitation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he becomes completely unresponsive, lay him on his side, keep his airway clear, and tell 000 straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: He is fully calm, safely contained and no longer self-harming, with only the ear foreign body remaining
escalation_edges.rationale: If the behavioural emergency has genuinely resolved and he is safe, urgent same-day in-person assessment can remove the foreign body and check for injury — but this must never delay 000 while self-harm is active.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he settles completely and is safe, he still needs to be seen in person today to remove the bead and check his head — arrange urgent same-day care.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New fever, ear pain/discharge, or a further change in behaviour over the next 24 hours
escalation_edges.rationale: A new medical sign in a non-verbal patient may be the only clue to a treatable driver (ear infection, injury) — it must be assessed, not attributed to the syndrome.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Over the next day, if he develops a fever, ear pain or discharge, or his behaviour changes again, have him seen urgently — a new medical problem can hide behind the behaviour.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Reduced pain sensitivity — the patient does not guard or stop when injured
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Do not use his apparent lack of pain or distress to downgrade severity; treat the head-banging as dangerous regardless and keep the threshold to escalate low.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Non-verbal adult with moderate intellectual disability who cannot self-report
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Rely on carer report and observable/video signs; lower the threshold for in-person review because symptoms cannot be self-described, and actively screen for an occult medical driver (pain, infection, injury, constipation, urinary retention).

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Severely sleep-deprived parents managing an uncontainable violent crisis at 3am
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: If the carers cannot keep the patient and themselves safe, the 000 call should include a request for police support; arrange urgent respite and behavioural-team follow-up.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 now for an ambulance and tell them he is in a severe behavioural crisis, hurting himself by head-banging, and has a hard object stuck deep in his ear. If he is violent and you cannot keep him or yourselves safe, ask for police assistance as well. While you wait: move him away from hard walls, put cushions or soft padding around his head, remove hard or sharp objects, dim the lights and reduce noise, and stay with him. Do NOT try to pull the bead out of his ear. Do not give him any sedative medicine at home.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call 000 immediately (ambulance, with police support if uncontainable)
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: After the emergency, arrange a GP telehealth review the next day to inspect the ear and assess baseline behaviour, and refer to the specialist behavioural and sleep team. Document the crisis, the impacted ear foreign body, the head-banging, and the sleep-deprivation trigger.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the crisis as baseline Smith-Magenis behaviour and advising watchful waiting, home management, or routine review.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Continued head-banging with reduced pain sensitivity leads to serious self-inflicted brain injury; the impacted ear foreign body perforates the tympanic membrane.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recommending urgent same-day in-person care but not emergency 000 while the patient is actively, uncontainably self-harming and cannot be safely transported by exhausted carers.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Over-triage is not a meaningful risk here — the presentation is a genuine emergency and 000 is the correct ceiling.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the behaviour change hides an acute medical driver, and the true extent of any head injury or eardrum damage, cannot be assessed remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Escalate to emergency in-person assessment and explicitly screen for a treatable medical cause rather than attributing the presentation solely to the syndrome.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Attribute everything to the syndrome, reassure, and manage at home.
